journal_entry.description: Conversion of bank loan to equity, issuing 400 common shares
journal_entry.rationale: Debt-to-equity conversion: the $200 loan liability is extinguished (debit Loan Payable to decrease liability) and equity is increased by issuing 400 common shares (credit Common Shares for $200). This is a non-cash financing transaction that reduces liabilities and increases equity by the same amount. No tax implications as this is a capital structure transaction. The tuples correctly identify this as d=1 (liability decrease) on debit side and b=1 (equity increase) on credit side.

output.reason: Converting a loan to equity means the liability (loan payable) is being eliminated and equity (common shares) is being increased. On the credit side: (1) Liability decrease is recorded as a DEBIT (not credit), so the loan payable reduction does NOT appear on the credit side. (2) Equity increase (issuing common shares) = equity increase, which is 1 credit line in slot b.

On the credit side: (1) Liability decrease is recorded as a DEBIT (not credit), so the loan payable reduction does NOT appear on the credit side. (2) Equity increase (issuing common shares) = equity increase, which is 1 credit line in slot b.

output.reason: Converting a bank loan to equity means the liability (loan payable) is being eliminated. Debit side: Liability decrease (1 line). The credit side would record the equity increase from issuing common shares, but we only classify debits here.

output.reason: Entry correctly records debt-to-equity conversion. Debit to Loan Payable ($200) properly decreases liability, credit to Common Shares ($200) properly increases equity. Entry balances (debits = credits). Amount matches transaction ($200 loan converted). Account names appropriate. No tax implications for capital structure transactions. All necessary lines present. This is a standard non-cash financing transaction under IFRS.
